segments.title: Harlech Castle sea supply and royal power
segments.summary: The Harlech lecture presents the castle as a sea-supplied fortress whose rock, water gate, garrison, and spectacle projected Edward I's power.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Harlech is harder to understand from its modern setting because the sea has retreated from the castle's original maritime edge. The water gate, rocky height, and layered defenses made the site formidable, while Irish sea supply during the 1294 siege shows why coastal access mattered to the Ring of Iron. He also treats Harlech as psychological power: a maintained, visible royal fortress that shaped local impressions of the English king even when people never saw him in person.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Harlech's original maritime setting, water gate, rocky construction site, and layered defensive position.
Dr. Clarke connects Harlech's value to sea supply, noting that fresh supplies reached the besieged castle from Ireland by sea.
The later discussion frames coastal castles as visible royal power and weighs Harlech's geography, structure, and small garrison.
